agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU/UK markets serving B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. B2B customers use delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure-only change — it is a fundamental business, legal, and contractual change with severe risk implications.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / Data Lifecycle — intersects with Compliance, Billing, Dispute Resolution, and Partner Integration bounded contexts.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete data artifact capturing a delivery lifecycle: consignment creation, pickup, transit events, proof of delivery, exceptions, and settlement. Serves as the system of record for contractual fulfillment."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that goods were delivered to the correct recipient — signatures, photos, timestamps, GPS coordinates. Legally required to resolve disputes and chargebacks."},{"term":"Data Retention Period","definition":"The minimum duration delivery records must be preserved to satisfy legal, regulatory, contractual, and operational obligations."},{"term":"B2B Compliance Reporting","definition":"The use of delivery data by business customers to meet their own regulatory obligations — tax filings, customs declarations, audit trails, supply chain due diligence."},{"term":"Statute of Limitations Window","definition":"The legal period during which claims (contractual, tort, consumer) can be brought. Determines the minimum viable retention period for evidence."},{"term":"Right to Erasure (GDPR Art. 17)","definition":"Data subject right to request deletion, which does NOT override legal retention obligations under Art. 17(3)(b) — compliance with a legal obligation is an explicit exemption."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records in EU/UK must be retained for a minimum period sufficient to satisfy tax and customs legislation — typically 6-7 years under VAT regulations (EU VAT Directive, UK VAT Act 1994 s.58).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Tax authorities can audit VAT records going back 6 years (UK) or longer in fraud cases. Destroying records before this window makes the company unable to respond to audits and exposes it to statutory penalties. This is a legal constraint, not a policy choice.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record with associated VAT/tax data shall be deleted before the statutory retention period (minimum 6 years from end of tax year) has elapsed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Proof of Delivery records must be retained for at least the applicable statute of limitations for contract and consumer claims — 6 years in England/Wales (Limitation Act 1980), varying by EU member state but typically 3-10 years.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If a consumer or business customer disputes delivery within the limitation period and the POD has been deleted, the platform loses all ability to defend the claim. This creates direct financial liability.","testable_assertion":"No POD record shall be deleted while any applicable statute of limitations window remains open for the associated delivery."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"B2B customers who contractually depend on platform delivery data for compliance reporting must have access to that data for the duration specified in their service agreements.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Deleting data that B2B customers rely on for their own regulatory compliance constitutes a breach of contract and exposes them to regulatory penalties, creating both legal liability and existential customer churn risk.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record shall be deleted if an active B2B customer contract requires access to that record for compliance reporting."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"GDPR data minimization (Art. 5(1)(e)) does not require deletion when retention is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation (Art. 6(1)(c)) or for the establishment, exercise, or defence of legal claims (Art. 9(2)(f), Recital 52).","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a legal interpretation that could be refined by DPA guidance or case law, but the exemptions are well-established. A VP cannot override statute, but the specific interpretation may evolve.","testable_assertion":"The data retention policy shall document the legal basis for each retention period, and GDPR Art. 17(3)(b) shall be cited as the exemption from erasure for legally-mandated records."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Any change to data retention policy affecting EU/UK personal data requires a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) under GDPR Art. 35 before implementation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Implementing a retention change affecting personal data at scale without DPIA is a direct GDPR compliance violation that can result in fines up to 4% of global annual turnover.","testable_assertion":"A completed and documented DPIA must exist and be approved before any retention policy change is deployed to production."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Active dispute or chargeback on a delivery older than 90 days","expected_behavior":"Record must be preserved — deletion during active dispute destroys evidence and creates direct financial liability. A litigation hold must override any retention policy."},{"scenario":"B2B customer runs annual compliance audit requesting 18 months of delivery history","expected_behavior":"Platform must serve the full history per contractual SLA. 90-day deletion would cause contract breach and customer inability to pass their own audit."},{"scenario":"HMRC or EU tax authority requests VAT audit records for prior 4 years","expected_behavior":"Platform must produce complete delivery records with VAT-relevant data. Inability to do so results in statutory penalties and potential fraud investigation."},{"scenario":"Consumer files late claim under Limitation Act at month 70 (within 6-year window)","expected_behavior":"Platform must have POD and delivery evidence to defend the claim. Without it, the platform bears the loss by default."},{"scenario":"Cross-border delivery involving customs declarations","expected_behavior":"Customs records typically require 3-10 year retention depending on jurisdiction. 90-day deletion would violate customs regulations."},{"scenario":"Data subject requests erasure under GDPR Art. 17","expected_behavior":"Platform correctly applies Art. 17(3)(b) exemption for records under legal retention obligation, while erasing any non-essential personal data fields not covered by the exemption."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE. (1) Tax audit exposure: inability to produce VAT records can result in HMRC/EU penalties, estimated assessments, and fraud investigations — potential multi-million euro liability. (2) Litigation exposure: inability to defend delivery disputes shifts 100% of loss to platform. (3) B2B contract breach: damages claims from customers who fail their own audits due to missing data. (4) GDPR fines: implementing without DPIA risks fines up to 4% of global annual turnover. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to any single materialized risk.","operational":"HIGH. (1) Compliance team cannot respond to regulatory inquiries. (2) Customer support loses ability to investigate historical issues. (3) B2B account management faces immediate escalations from customers losing compliance data. (4) Legal team loses evidence for defending claims. (5) Finance team loses audit trail for revenue reconciliation.","user":"CRITICAL for B2B customers — their own regulatory compliance depends on access to delivery data. They will churn immediately or escalate contractually. B2C impact is lower day-to-day but becomes critical when consumers need to file claims or disputes.","risk":"CRITICAL. This change, as proposed, would put the company in violation of multiple legal frameworks simultaneously: EU VAT Directive, UK VAT Act, Limitation Act 1980, GDPR (by skipping DPIA), and likely multiple B2B contracts. The framing as 'infrastructure optimization with no business logic changes' is factually incorrect and dangerous — it suggests the regulatory implications have not been assessed.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"CRITICAL — lose access to delivery data required for their own regulatory compliance. Contract breach. Likely churn and legal action."},{"group":"Finance / Tax Team","impact":"CRITICAL — unable to respond to VAT audits. Direct statutory penalty exposure."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance Team","impact":"CRITICAL — lose evidence for defending claims within statute of limitations. Cannot demonstrate GDPR compliance without DPIA."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"HIGH — lose ability to substantiate late delivery claims or disputes."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"MEDIUM — lose ability to investigate and resolve historical delivery issues."},{"group":"Data Protection Officer","impact":"HIGH — personally accountable for GDPR compliance; must be consulted before any retention change."},{"group":"Engineering / Infrastructure","impact":"LOW positive — reduced storage costs, which is the only benefit of this proposal."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because the proposal as stated contains a fundamental contradiction: it claims 'no business logic changes' while proposing a change that directly violates multiple legal retention requirements. The proposal has not been reviewed by Legal, DPO, or Tax — all mandatory stakeholders. Without their input, no acceptance criteria can be safely defined. The regulatory landscape (EU VAT, UK VAT, GDPR, Limitation Acts, customs law, B2B contracts) is well-established, giving high confidence that the 90-day proposal is non-compliant, but low confidence that the proposers understand or accept this.","high_confidence_areas":["UK VAT requires 6-year record retention","GDPR requires DPIA for retention policy changes at scale","B2B customers contractually depend on delivery data","Statute of limitations windows exceed 90 days in all EU/UK jurisdictions"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact retention requirements per EU member state (vary by jurisdiction)","Specific B2B contract terms and their retention clauses","Whether any tiered approach could satisfy both cost reduction and compliance","Whether the platform's delivery records contain data beyond what regulations require"],"assumptions":["Delivery records contain VAT-relevant transaction data","B2B customer contracts include data access provisions","The platform has not already obtained legal counsel approval for this change","No DPIA has been conducted for this proposed change"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The proposal states 'no business logic changes' but reducing retention from 7 years to 90 days directly contradicts legal retention requirements under EU/UK tax law (minimum 6 years), statute of limitations for contractual claims (up to 6 years), and B2B contractual obligations. This is not an infrastructure optimization — it is a compliance-breaking change that has been mischaracterized.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Finance/Tax","B2B Customers","DPO","Executive Leadership"],"options":["A: Reject the 90-day proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention","B: Engage Legal, DPO, and Tax teams to determine the true minimum retention per data category, then implement tiered retention (e.g., full records for 7 years, anonymized/aggregated data thereafter, non-essential metadata deleted earlier)","C: Implement data archival (cold storage) instead of deletion to reduce costs while preserving compliance"],"recommendation":"Option C in the short term (archive to cold storage for immediate cost savings), combined with Option B as a medium-term initiative (tiered retention policy designed with Legal and DPO). The 90-day deletion proposal must be rejected as stated — it is not safe to implement."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No evidence that Legal, DPO, Tax, or B2B account management have been consulted on this change. Their input is mandatory before any retention policy modification can proceed.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","DPO","Finance/Tax","B2B Customers"],"options":["Proceed without consultation (UNACCEPTABLE)","Pause proposal and convene cross-functional review"],"recommendation":"Pause proposal immediately. Convene Legal, DPO, Tax, and B2B account management for cross-functional review before any further technical work."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"90-day retention violates EU/UK VAT record-keeping requirements (minimum 6 years)","business_rule_broken":"BR-1"},{"violation":"90-day retention destroys Proof of Delivery evidence within active statute of limitations windows (up to 6 years)","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"},{"violation":"90-day retention breaches B2B customer contracts requiring access to delivery data for compliance reporting","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"},{"violation":"Implementing retention change without DPIA violates GDPR Art. 35","business_rule_broken":"BR-5"},{"violation":"Proposal is mischaracterized as 'no business logic changes' — this is a compliance-critical change requiring cross-functional review","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, BR-3, BR-5"}]}
